confusions.reason: Both shots involve a wristy deflection off the pads toward the leg side. The pose geometry at the point of contact is nearly identical — the bat angle and hip rotation differ by only a few degrees. The main distinction is bat face angle, which MediaPipe's skeletal landmarks cannot capture.

These are essentially the same shot with different naming conventions in the dataset. Both involve a horizontal bat swing to a short-pitched delivery at chest height, resulting in identical skeletal poses.

Both are cross-bat horizontal shots to short-pitched deliveries. The Hook is played to balls at head height (arms higher), while the Pull targets chest height. In many frames, the difference in arm elevation is marginal, especially mid-swing when the bat is still rising.

Both are front-foot drives with a straight bat. The key difference is the direction of the shot — cover drives go through the off side while straight drives go back past the bowler. However, the body pose and bat swing path are extremely similar; only the final follow-through angle differs.

Both involve an elevated bat swing with the batsman's weight transferring forward. The difference is the direction of the loft — these are mirror shots. Skeletal landmarks of the upper body are nearly symmetrical, making it difficult for the model to distinguish direction without ball trajectory context.

The generic 'drive' class overlaps with Cover Drive as both involve classical front-foot driving technique. Cover Drive is specifically off-side, while 'drive' is a broader category. The pose similarity makes separation challenging.

Both are played from a low, kneeling position against spin bowling. The Reverse Sweep involves a grip change and inverted bat face, but the lower body pose (kneeling, front foot planted) is identical. The model relies heavily on upper body landmarks which may not clearly capture the grip reversal.

Both are cut shots played to short deliveries outside off stump. The Square Cut is played in front of the wicket with more power, while the Late Cut is played later and finer. The timing difference means the body positions at contact are similar, differing mainly in how far back the hands are — a subtle cue.
